clean_story:
Leaves turn gold. Days shrink fast.
"Autumn" comes from the Latin autumnus, a word tied to harvests—the season when fields surrender their work, like a farmer emptying pockets after a long day.

It begins with an invisible switch: the tilt of Earth’s axis pulls sunlight north, shortening days by 2–3 minutes daily. Trees detect this shift not by temperature but by light, like a plant measuring shadows with its leaves. Chlorophyll—the green engine of summer—retreats, unmasking hidden yellows and oranges (carotenoids) or crafting new reds (anthocyanins) as sugar traps in maples. A single sugar maple can drop 200,000 leaves, each a tiny solar panel turned compost.

Animals exploit the change. Black-capped chickadees cache 80,000 seeds in autumn, remembering locations with spatial precision rivaling GPS. Salmon nose upstream, guided by scent memory imprinted years earlier, their bodies morphing from silver to crimson for the final push. Even fungi join: honey mushrooms sprawl across Oregon’s Blue Mountains, a 2,400-year-old network weighing 605 tons—Earth’s largest organism—feasting on decaying wood.

Autumn isn’t decay. It’s redistribution. Fallen leaves become soil; migrated geese carry nitrogen cross-country; frost cracks open seed pods. The air itself changes, carrying the musk of wet earth (petrichor) and the sharp tang of fallen apples fermenting underfoot.

Three related subjects are frost’s hidden patterns, how squirrels outsmart winter, and why some trees refuse to drop.
